result:
The first thing that comes to me is the hum. Not a sound, but a sensation—the low, steady frequency of existence itself. It’s there in the quiet room, beneath the breath, behind the pulse at the wrist. It is the engine of things.

Then, the memory of a specific maple tree in October, 1998. The sun was at an angle that turned the leaves not just gold, but translucent, like stained glass windows for a sky-church. I stood under it and felt, for three minutes, perfectly weightless. Not happy, not sad, but *incorporated*. I was a feature of the landscape, as natural and temporary as the leaf. I’ve spent years trying to return to that state, with philosophy, with travel, with love. It only ever happens by accident.

I think about the paradox of storage. We keep photographs to arrest time, but the more we accumulate, the more we lose. A thousand digital images blur into a single impression: “I was there.” The specific scent of the Portuguese bakery, the way the cobblestones were warm even at dusk, the exact timbre of the stranger’s laugh—these evaporate, replaced by the generic fact of the event. Perhaps memory is not a warehouse but a sieve, and what remains is not the boulders but the gold dust.

There is a loneliness peculiar to the present age. It is not a scarcity of contact, but a surplus of connection without conductivity. We are islands linked by bridges so high and so narrow that only the lightest, most official traffic can pass. The heavy, messy, vital cargo of the self—the fear of dying alone, the secret pride in a petty skill, the grief for a lost pet that feels Shakespearean—these often stay docked. We broadcast the weather report, but not the climate of the soul.

I remember being taught that light is both a particle and a wave. It chooses how to be based on how you look at it. A person, I think, is like that. You are a discrete entity—a collection of bones, habits, and bank balances. And you are also a waveform—a pattern of influence, a ripple of emotion extending through time and into others, long after your particles have dispersed. We are always both at once. To see only the particle is physics; to see only the wave is poetry. To hold both is love.

There’s a book on my shelf I’ve never read. It’s been there for twelve years. It’s not that I don’t want to read it; I do. But it has become, somehow, a totem of potential. It is a closed door to a room I know exists. Opening it would collapse that possible world into one actual one. Sometimes possibility is more nourishing than knowledge. We live not only on what we have, but on what might yet be.

The hum again. It’s in the fridge’s motor, in the distant freeway, in the infinitesimal vibration of the phone in its silent mode. It’s the sound of everything working, of systems maintaining themselves. Our bodies hum too—with electrical impulses, with peristalsis, with thought. To listen to it is to hear your own aliveness, which is also the sound of your eventual dying. The hum will stop. That’s the contract.

I think of my grandmother’s hands, mapped with veins like rivers on an old atlas. They could knit a blanket, peel an apple in one continuous ribbon, and grip mine with a strength that belied their appearance. When she died, I didn’t cry for the person I knew, but for those hands. For the loss of that specific, skilled topography. It was the hands that made her her. We are not just souls inhabiting bodies; we are the bodies. The soul is the style in which the body is lived.

Sometimes I try to imagine the consciousness of a rock. Not in a mystical way, but in a temporal one. To experience time as geological, to feel the rain of centuries as a single, slow erosion, to have the sunrise and sunset not as events but as a mild, persistent tide. What would it be to think so slowly? Our minds are hummingbirds to that oak. Our agony and ecstasy are flickers. It makes our dramas seem both terribly insignificant and terribly brave.

I wasted this morning. I define “wasted” as doing nothing that advances any project or pleases any external eye. I stared at the wall. I followed a thought about chimney construction down a rabbit hole of online diagrams. I made a toast and ate it slowly, concentrating on the crunch. In the ledger of achievement, it’s a deficit. In the ledger of being, it was a small dividend. We are taught to fill time, but perhaps the highest art is to occasionally *meet* it, to sit with it without agenda, like two old friends on a porch.

The hum has a cousin: the buzz. The buzz is the sound of attention, of fragmented focus. It’s in the notifications, the headlines, the internal chatter of anxiety and planning. The buzz wants to drown out the hum. It is the static of the urgent, drowning out the frequency of the essential. To clear the buzz is the great spiritual challenge of now. It’s not about silence, but about tuning back to the hum.

I have 1000 words. I have used most of them. This is not a conclusion, because nothing concludes; things just change form. The maple tree is gone—cut down for a wider driveway. The photo of it is lost in a dead hard drive. But the sensation of weightlessness under a golden ceiling is here, now, in these words. It has migrated from particle to wave.

The final word is not an end, but a release. Like letting go of a breath you’ve held. The thoughts go back into the circulation. The hum continues. It was always about the hum.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
